content:
# Exodus Chapter 27 -- The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios ~48r-48v (PDF pp. 95-96); Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 69-70 (LXX) | **Source Image:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_027_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_027_page_002.jpg`; `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/EXO_027_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_027_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

> **MANUSCRIPT NOTE -- THE OUTER PRECINCTS:** The Leningrad Codex presents Exodus 27 in standard prose column format with full Masoretic vocalization and cantillation. This chapter moves outward from the tabernacle structure of chapter 26 to describe the outer precincts: the bronze altar for burnt offerings (vv. 1-8), the linen-walled courtyard enclosure (vv. 9-19), and the perpetual oil for the lampstand (vv. 20-21). The movement is architecturally logical -- having described the dwelling itself, the text now addresses the open-air precinct surrounding it. The bronze altar is the first object encountered upon entering the courtyard, the place where Israel's sacrificial worship is enacted in public space. The courtyard hangings create the boundary between sacred and common ground. The chapter closes with the oil commandment (vv. 20-21), which shifts from architecture to perpetual liturgical practice -- the first ongoing obligation specified in the tabernacle instructions. The Vaticanus manuscript (pp. 69-70) presents the LXX in continuous uncial script; the LXX versification aligns with the MT throughout this chapter.

---

## The Bronze Altar (vv. 1-8)

**27:1** "And you shall make the altar of acacia wood: five cubits the length and five cubits the width; the altar shall be square, and three cubits its height. [fn1]

**27:2** And you shall make its horns on its four corners; from it shall its horns be, and you shall overlay it with bronze. [fn2]

**27:3** And you shall make its pots for removing its ashes, and its shovels, and its basins, and its forks, and its firepans; all its vessels you shall make of bronze. [fn3]

**27:4** And you shall make for it a grating, a network of bronze mesh, and you shall make upon the network four rings of bronze on its four ends. [fn4]

**27:5** And you shall set it under the ledge of the altar below, and the network shall reach to the middle of the altar. [fn5]

**27:6** And you shall make poles for the altar, poles of acacia wood, and you shall overlay them with bronze. [fn6]

**27:7** And its poles shall be put into the rings, and the poles shall be on the two sides of the altar when it is carried. [fn7]

**27:8** Hollow with boards you shall make it; as He showed you on the mountain, so shall they make it. [fn8]

---

## The Courtyard of the Tabernacle (vv. 9-19)

**27:9** "And you shall make the courtyard of the tabernacle: for the south side, southward, hangings for the courtyard of fine twisted linen, one hundred cubits in length for one side; [fn9]

**27:10** and its pillars twenty, and their sockets twenty, of bronze; the hooks of the pillars and their bands of silver. [fn10]

**27:11** And likewise for the north side in length, hangings one hundred cubits in length, and its pillars twenty, and their sockets twenty, of bronze; the hooks of the pillars and their bands of silver. [fn11]

**27:12** And the width of the courtyard on the west side: hangings of fifty cubits; their pillars ten and their sockets ten. [fn12]

**27:13** And the width of the courtyard on the east side, eastward: fifty cubits. [fn13]

**27:14** And the hangings for the one shoulder shall be fifteen cubits; their pillars three and their sockets three. [fn14]

**27:15** And for the second shoulder, hangings of fifteen cubits; their pillars three and their sockets three. [fn15]

**27:16** And for the gate of the courtyard, a screen of twenty cubits, of blue and purple and scarlet yarn and fine twisted linen, the work of an embroiderer; their pillars four and their sockets four. [fn16]

**27:17** All the pillars of the courtyard all around shall be banded with silver; their hooks of silver and their sockets of bronze. [fn17]

**27:18** The length of the courtyard shall be one hundred cubits, and the width fifty by fifty, and the height five cubits of fine twisted linen, and their sockets of bronze. [fn18]

**27:19** All the vessels of the tabernacle for all its service, and all its pegs, and all the pegs of the courtyard -- bronze. [fn19]

---

## The Oil for the Lamp (vv. 20-21)

**27:20** "And you shall command the Sons of Israel that they bring to you pure oil of beaten olives for the light, to cause a lamp to go up continually. [fn20]

**27:21** In the tent of meeting, outside the veil which is before the testimony, Aaron and his sons shall arrange it from evening until morning before YHWH -- a statute forever for their generations, from the Sons of Israel. [fn21]

---

## Translator's Notes

### Structural Note

Exodus 27 completes the outward movement begun in chapter 25. Where chapter 25 described the innermost furnishings (ark, table, lampstand) and chapter 26 the tabernacle structure itself, chapter 27 addresses the outer precinct -- the bronze altar, the courtyard enclosure, and the perpetual lamp oil. The architectural logic radiates concentrically from YHWH's throne outward to the boundary between sacred and common space.

The chapter divides into three sections: (1) the bronze altar of burnt offering (vv. 1-8), the largest piece of tabernacle furniture and the dominant feature of the courtyard; (2) the courtyard enclosure defined by its hangings, pillars, and gate (vv. 9-19); and (3) the perpetual lamp oil command (vv. 20-21), which shifts from architecture to liturgical practice and introduces Aaron and the priesthood into the instruction narrative for the first time. The material hierarchy established in chapters 25-26 reaches its completion here: gold dominates the Holy of Holies and Holy Place; bronze governs the courtyard. Every structural element in the outer precinct is bronze -- the metal of the threshold between sacred and profane.

### Key Decisions

**"Sockets" for *adanim* (throughout):** EMET renders "sockets," LOGOS renders "bases," PNEUMA renders "bases." The consensus follows EMET -- "sockets" for *adanim* (from *eden*, "base, pedestal") best captures the functional role of these bronze pedestals as receiving housings into which the pillar bases are inserted, consistent with the decision established at 26:19.

**"Hangings" for *qela'im* (vv. 9-15):** EMET renders "hangings," LOGOS renders "hangings," PNEUMA renders "curtains." The consensus follows EMET/LOGOS -- "hangings" for *qela'im* (from *qala*, "to sling, hang") distinguishes these plain linen panels of the courtyard from the *yeri'ot* ("curtains") of the tabernacle structure (ch. 26). PNEUMA's "curtains" collapses the distinction between two different Hebrew terms for two different textile functions.

**"Shoulder" for *katef* (vv. 14-15):** EMET renders "shoulder," LOGOS renders "shoulder," PNEUMA renders "side of the entrance." The consensus follows EMET/LOGOS -- "shoulder" preserves the Hebrew architectural metaphor (*katef*, literally "shoulder"), which designates the flanking sections on either side of the gate. PNEUMA's paraphrase clarifies the meaning but loses the characteristic Hebrew body-metaphor for architectural features.

**"Bands" for *khashaqeihem* (vv. 10-11, 17):** EMET renders "bands," LOGOS renders "bands," PNEUMA renders "bands." All three translators agree. The *khashaqim* (from *khashaq*, "to bind, attach") are silver bands or fillets encircling the courtyard pillars.

**"Screen" for *masakh* (v. 16):** All three translators agree on "screen." Consistent with the decision at 26:36 -- the *masakh* is distinguished from the *parokhet* (inner veil) by craft (*roqem*-work, not *choshev*-work) and by function (entrance covering, not inner sanctuary divider).

**"Removing its ashes" for *le-dashno* (v. 3):** EMET renders "for removing its ashes," LOGOS renders "to receive its ashes," PNEUMA renders "for removing ashes." The consensus follows EMET/PNEUMA -- "for removing its ashes" reflects the piel force of *dashan* ("to remove fat-ashes"), an active process of ash removal rather than passive reception.

**"Grating" for *mikhbar* (v. 4):** All three translators agree on "grating." The noun *mikhbar* (from *kavar*, "to interweave") denotes an interwoven mesh or lattice, further specified by *reshet* ("network"). The consensus renders the full phrase as "a grating, a network of bronze mesh."

**"Ledge" for *karkob* (v. 5):** EMET renders "ledge," LOGOS renders "ledge," PNEUMA renders "ledge." All three agree. The *karkob* is a projecting rim or structural ledge around the altar, apparently at or below the midpoint.

**Word order for *shemen zayit zakh katit* (v. 20):** EMET renders "oil of olives, pure, beaten." LOGOS renders "pure oil of beaten olives." PNEUMA renders "pure oil of pressed olives." The consensus follows LOGOS -- "pure oil of beaten olives" produces the most natural English while preserving all four elements: *shemen* ("oil"), *zayit* ("olive"), *zakh* ("pure"), *katit* ("beaten"). EMET's Hebrew-order rendering, while literal, is awkward in English. PNEUMA's "pressed" for *katit* is less precise than "beaten" -- *katit* (from *katat*, "to beat, crush") specifically denotes hand-crushing in a mortar, not mechanical pressing.

**"Arrange" for *ya'arokh* (v. 21):** All three translators agree on "arrange" (EMET/LOGOS) or "keep burning" (PNEUMA). The consensus follows EMET/LOGOS -- "arrange" for *ya'arokh* (qal imperfect of *arakh*, "to arrange, set in order") preserves the priestly technical term for tending the lamp. PNEUMA's "keep burning" interprets the result rather than translating the action.

**"Statute forever" for *khuqqat olam* (v. 21):** EMET renders "a statute of perpetuity," LOGOS renders "a statute forever," PNEUMA renders "a lasting ordinance." The consensus follows LOGOS -- "a statute forever" is the most natural English rendering of the Hebrew construct chain *khuqqat olam*. EMET's "of perpetuity" is unnecessarily formal, and PNEUMA's "lasting ordinance" replaces *khuqqah* ("statute, inscribed decree") with the weaker "ordinance."

### Vaticanus (LXX) Divergences

The Vaticanus text of Exodus 27 (pp. 69-70) preserves the LXX rendering with several notable divergences from the MT: (1) In v. 3, the LXX introduces a *stephane* ("rim, crown") for the altar before the utensils list, corresponding to the *karkob* of v. 5 but relocated -- the LXX's utensil order differs from the MT's arrangement. (2) In v. 14, the LXX renders the hangings measurement with *hupsos* ("height") where the MT has length, a persistent interpretive difference in the courtyard dimensions. (3) In v. 18, the LXX reads "a hundred by a hundred" (*hekaton eph' hekaton*) where the MT has "fifty by fifty" (*khamishim ba-khamishim*) -- a significant numerical divergence. (4) In v. 21, the LXX renders *ohel mo'ed* as *skene tou marturiou* ("tent of testimony") rather than "tent of meeting," interpreting *mo'ed* through the lens of *edut* ("testimony"). (5) Throughout, the LXX maintains its characteristic rendering of *shittim* as *asepta* ("incorruptible") and *nekhoshet* as *chalkos* ("bronze/copper"). (6) In v. 21, the LXX uses *kurios* ("Lord") for the Tetragrammaton YHWH, following the standard Septuagintal convention.

---

> **[fn1] v.1 -- *Ve-asita et-ha-mizbeakh atsei shittim khamesh ammot orekh ve-khamesh ammot rokhav ravua yihyeh ha-mizbeakh ve-shalosh ammot qomato*: "And you shall make the altar of acacia wood: five cubits the length and five cubits the width; the altar shall be square, and three cubits its height"** -- All three translators agree in substance. EMET and LOGOS preserve the Hebrew word order closely; PNEUMA restructures to natural English ("Build the altar out of acacia wood"). The consensus follows EMET/LOGOS, preserving the Hebrew clause structure. The altar's dimensions (5 x 5 x 3 cubits, approximately 7.5 x 7.5 x 4.5 feet) make it the largest piece of furniture in the tabernacle complex. *Ravua* ("square," qal passive participle of *rava*, "to make square") -- the altar is a perfect square in plan. The LXX renders *mizbeakh* as *thusiastērion* ("altar, place of sacrifice").

> **[fn2] v.2 -- *Ve-asita qarnotav al arba pinnotav mimmenu tihyeynah qarnotav ve-tsippita oto nekhoshet*: "And you shall make its horns on its four corners; from it shall its horns be, and you shall overlay it with bronze"** -- All three translators agree. *Qarnotav* ("its horns," from *qeren*, "horn") -- projecting horn-shaped extensions at each corner of the altar. The crucial phrase *mimmenu tihyeynah qarnotav* ("from it shall its horns be") specifies that the horns are of one piece with the altar itself, not separate attachments -- paralleling the cherubim beaten "from" the *kapporet* (25:19) and the branches "from" the lampstand (25:33). The horns serve as the point of blood application in sin offerings (Lev 4:7, 18, 25, 30) and as a place of asylum (1 Kgs 1:50; 2:28). The LXX renders *qarnot* as *kerata* ("horns").

> **[fn3] v.3 -- *Ve-asita sirotav le-dashno ve-ya'av u-mizraqotav u-mizlegotav u-makhtotav le-khol kelav ta'aseh nekhoshet*: "And you shall make its pots for removing its ashes, and its shovels, and its basins, and its forks, and its firepans; all its vessels you shall make of bronze"** -- All three translators agree in substance. The utensil list: *sirotav* ("its pots," for collecting ash), *ya'av* ("its shovels," flat scoops for handling ash), *mizraqotav* ("its basins," for catching and tossing sacrificial blood), *mizlegotav* ("its forks," three-pronged instruments for handling sacrificial flesh), *makhtotav* ("its firepans," censers or fire-carrying pans). PNEUMA adds "for catching the blood" to the basins -- accurate but interpretive; the consensus omits the gloss. The LXX diverges in order here, inserting the altar rim (*stephane*) before the utensils.

> **[fn4] v.4 -- *Ve-asita lo mikhbar ma'aseh reshet nekhoshet ve-asita al-ha-reshet arba tabb'ot nekhoshet al arba qetsotav*: "And you shall make for it a grating, a network of bronze mesh, and you shall make upon the network four rings of bronze on its four ends"** -- All three translators agree. *Mikhbar* ("grating," from *kavar*, "to interweave") -- an interwoven mesh or lattice. *Reshet* ("net, network") further specifies the construction. The grating is fitted with four bronze rings at its corners, which serve as attachment points for the carrying poles. The LXX renders *mikhbar* as *eschara* ("hearth, grating") and *reshet* as *diktuōton* ("netlike").

> **[fn5] v.5 -- *Ve-natattah otah takhat karkob ha-mizbeakh mi-lemattah ve-haytah ha-reshet ad khatsi ha-mizbeakh*: "And you shall set it under the ledge of the altar below, and the network shall reach to the middle of the altar"** -- EMET renders "you shall put it." LOGOS renders "you shall set it." PNEUMA renders "Set the mesh." The consensus follows LOGOS -- "set" for *ve-natattah* captures the deliberate positioning. *Karkob* ("ledge, rim") -- a projecting structural ledge around the altar, apparently at or near the midpoint. The grating extends from below this ledge to the altar's midpoint. The exact placement of both the *karkob* and the *reshet* is debated among interpreters.

> **[fn6] v.6 -- *Ve-asita baddim la-mizbeakh baddei atsei shittim ve-tsippita otam nekhoshet*: "And you shall make poles for the altar, poles of acacia wood, and you shall overlay them with bronze"** -- All three translators agree. *Baddim* ("poles, carrying-bars") -- the same term used for the ark's poles (25:13-15) and the table's poles (25:28), but here overlaid with bronze rather than gold, consistent with the material hierarchy: gold for inner furnishings, bronze for the courtyard altar.

> **[fn7] v.7 -- *Ve-huvah et-baddav ba-tabb'ot ve-hayu ha-baddim al shnei tsal'ot ha-mizbeakh bi-s'et oto*: "And its poles shall be put into the rings, and the poles shall be on the two sides of the altar when it is carried"** -- All three translators agree. *Ve-huvah* (hophal perfect of *bo*, "to come, bring") -- "shall be brought/put"; the poles are inserted into the rings. *Bi-s'et* (qal infinitive construct of *nasa*, "to lift, carry") -- "when carrying, when lifting"; the poles are for transporting the altar during Israel's wilderness journeys.

> **[fn8] v.8 -- *Nevuv lukhot ta'aseh oto ka'asher her'ah otekha ba-har ken ya'asu*: "Hollow with boards you shall make it; as He showed you on the mountain, so shall they make it"** -- All three translators agree. *Nevuv* ("hollow," qal passive participle of *navav*, "to hollow out") -- the altar is not solid but a hollow frame of planks, presumably filled with earth when set up (cf. 20:24). *Lukhot* ("boards, planks") -- the same word used for the tablets of the covenant (24:12), but here denoting flat planks forming the hollow box. The closing formula *ka'asher her'ah otekha ba-har ken ya'asu* echoes 25:9, 40 and 26:30, reaffirming the heavenly pattern. EMET renders "he showed"; LOGOS capitalizes "He showed." The consensus capitalizes, consistent with the divine subject.

> **[fn9] v.9 -- *Ve-asita et khatsar ha-mishkan li-f'at negev teimanah qela'im le-khatser shesh moshzar me'ah va-ammah orekh la-pe'ah ha-ekhat*: "And you shall make the courtyard of the tabernacle: for the south side, southward, hangings for the courtyard of fine twisted linen, one hundred cubits in length for one side"** -- EMET renders "hangings." LOGOS renders "hangings." PNEUMA renders "curtains." The consensus follows EMET/LOGOS (see Key Decisions, "hangings" for *qela'im*). *Khatsar* ("courtyard," from *khatsar*, "to enclose") -- the open-air rectangular enclosure surrounding the tabernacle. *Negev teimanah* -- a double directional marker: *negev* ("south, dry land") + *teimanah* ("southward, to the right hand"). The south side is 100 cubits long (approximately 150 feet). The LXX renders *khatsar* as *aule* ("courtyard") and *qela'im* as *istia* ("sails, canvas").

> **[fn10] v.10 -- *Ve-ammudav esrim ve-adneihem esrim nekhoshet vavei ha-ammudim va-khashaqeihem kasef*: "And its pillars twenty, and their sockets twenty, of bronze; the hooks of the pillars and their bands of silver"** -- All three translators agree. The courtyard pillar composition: bronze sockets at the base, silver hooks and bands on the pillars. *Khashaqeihem* ("their bands/fillets," from *khashaq*, "to bind") -- silver bands encircling the pillars.

> **[fn11] v.11 -- *Ve-khen li-f'at tsafon ba-orekh qela'im me'ah orekh ve-ammudav esrim ve-adneihem esrim nekhoshet vavei ha-ammudim va-khashaqeihem kasef*: "And likewise for the north side in length, hangings one hundred cubits in length, and its pillars twenty, and their sockets twenty, of bronze; the hooks of the pillars and their bands of silver"** -- All three translators agree. The north side mirrors the south side exactly: 100 cubits of hangings, twenty pillars, twenty bronze sockets, silver hooks and bands.

> **[fn12] v.12 -- *Ve-rokhav he-khatser li-f'at yam qela'im khamishim ammah ammudeihem asarah ve-adneihem asarah*: "And the width of the courtyard on the west side: hangings of fifty cubits; their pillars ten and their sockets ten"** -- All three translators agree. The west side (rear) is 50 cubits (approximately 75 feet). *Yam* ("sea") = "west" -- the same directional convention as 26:22. The courtyard is a 100 x 50 cubit rectangle.

> **[fn13] v.13 -- *Ve-rokhav he-khatser li-f'at qedmah mizrakhah khamishim ammah*: "And the width of the courtyard on the east side, eastward: fifty cubits"** -- All three translators agree. *Qedmah mizrakhah* ("eastward, toward the sunrise") -- a double directional marker, paralleling *negev teimanah* in v. 9. The east side is also 50 cubits total, divided into three sections (vv. 14-16).

> **[fn14] v.14 -- *Va-khamesh esreh ammah qela'im la-katef ammudeihem sheloshah ve-adneihem sheloshah*: "And the hangings for the one shoulder shall be fifteen cubits; their pillars three and their sockets three"** -- EMET renders "shoulder." LOGOS renders "shoulder." PNEUMA renders "one side of the entrance." The consensus follows EMET/LOGOS (see Key Decisions, "shoulder" for *katef*). The east side is divided: 15-cubit hangings on each shoulder (vv. 14-15), plus a 20-cubit gate screen in the center (v. 16), totaling 50 cubits.

> **[fn15] v.15 -- *Ve-la-katef ha-shenit khamesh esreh qela'im ammudeihem sheloshah ve-adneihem sheloshah*: "And for the second shoulder, hangings of fifteen cubits; their pillars three and their sockets three"** -- All three translators agree. The second shoulder mirrors the first: 15 cubits of hangings flanking the other side of the gate.

> **[fn16] v.16 -- *U-le-sha'ar he-khatser masakh esrim ammah tekhelet ve-argaman ve-tola'at shani ve-shesh moshzar ma'aseh roqem ammudeihem arba'ah ve-adneihem arba'ah*: "And for the gate of the courtyard, a screen of twenty cubits, of blue and purple and scarlet yarn and fine twisted linen, the work of an embroiderer; their pillars four and their sockets four"** -- All three translators agree. The gate screen employs the same four colored materials as the tabernacle entrance screen (26:36) and the parokhet (26:31), but like the entrance screen, it is *ma'aseh roqem* ("work of an embroiderer"), not *ma'aseh choshev* ("work of a skilled craftsman"). The textile hierarchy encodes graduated holiness: embroidered gate screen (outermost) < embroidered entrance screen (middle) < woven veil with cherubim (innermost).

> **[fn17] v.17 -- *Kol ammudei he-khatser saviv mekhushshaqim kesef vaveihem kesef ve-adneihem nekhoshet*: "All the pillars of the courtyard all around shall be banded with silver; their hooks of silver and their sockets of bronze"** -- All three translators agree. *Mekhushshaqim* (pual participle of *khashaq*, "to bind, band") -- "banded, filleted." Summary of the courtyard pillar materials: bronze sockets at the base, silver bands and hooks above. The LXX renders *mekhushshaqim* as *katerguromenoi* ("silver-plated"), a more expansive interpretation.

> **[fn18] v.18 -- *Orekh he-khatser me'ah va-ammah ve-rokhav khamishim ba-khamishim ve-qomah khamesh ammot shesh moshzar ve-adneihem nekhoshet*: "The length of the courtyard shall be one hundred cubits, and the width fifty by fifty, and the height five cubits of fine twisted linen, and their sockets of bronze"** -- All three translators agree. *Khamishim ba-khamishim* ("fifty by fifty") -- an unusual expression; it may mean "fifty at each end, consistently fifty" or "fifty on each side." The courtyard dimensions summarized: 100 cubits long, 50 cubits wide, 5 cubits high. The hangings are 5 cubits tall (approximately 7.5 feet). Notable LXX divergence: the LXX reads "a hundred by a hundred" (*hekaton eph' hekaton*) where the MT has "fifty by fifty."

> **[fn19] v.19 -- *Le-khol kelei ha-mishkan be-khol avodato ve-khol yetedotav ve-khol yitdot he-khatser nekhoshet*: "All the vessels of the tabernacle for all its service, and all its pegs, and all the pegs of the courtyard -- bronze"** -- EMET renders "pegs." LOGOS renders "tent pegs." PNEUMA renders "tent pegs." The consensus follows EMET -- "pegs" for *yetedot* (from *yated*, "peg, pin") is sufficient; the context makes their function clear. This verse serves as a summary: everything in the outer precinct -- utilitarian implements and structural fasteners alike -- is bronze.

> **[fn20] v.20 -- *Ve-attah tetsavveh et-benei Yisrael ve-yiqkhu eleikha shemen zayit zakh katit la-ma'or le-ha'alot ner tamid*: "And you shall command the Sons of Israel that they bring to you pure oil of beaten olives for the light, to cause a lamp to go up continually"** -- EMET renders "oil of olives, pure, beaten." LOGOS renders "pure oil of beaten olives." PNEUMA renders "pure oil of pressed olives." The consensus follows LOGOS (see Key Decisions, word order for *shemen zayit zakh katit*). *Ve-attah* ("and you") -- emphatic pronoun, placing personal responsibility on Moses. *Zakh* ("pure, clear") -- free of sediment; *katit* ("beaten," from *katat*, "to beat, crush") -- the finest grade of olive oil, produced by hand-crushing in a mortar rather than pressing in an oil press. *Le-ha'alot ner* ("to cause a lamp to go up") -- hiphil infinitive construct of *alah* ("to go up"); lighting the lamp is described as "causing it to ascend," reflecting the flame's upward movement. *Tamid* ("continually") -- the lamp is to burn as an ongoing obligation across all generations. This verse introduces the root of the parashah name *Tetsavveh* (Exod 27:20-30:10).

> **[fn21] v.21 -- *Be-ohel mo'ed mi-khuts la-parokhet asher al-ha-edut ya'arokh oto Aharon u-vanav me-erev ad-boqer lifnei YHWH khuqqat olam le-dorotam me-et benei Yisrael*: "In the tent of meeting, outside the veil which is before the testimony, Aaron and his sons shall arrange it from evening until morning before YHWH -- a statute forever for their generations, from the Sons of Israel"** -- All three translators agree in substance. *Ohel mo'ed* ("tent of meeting") -- the first use of this full designation in the tabernacle instructions; *mo'ed* (from *ya'ad*, "to appoint, meet") names the tabernacle by its relational function: not merely a dwelling (*mishkan*) but a place of appointed encounter. *Ya'arokh* (qal imperfect of *arakh*, "to arrange") -- "he shall arrange"; the singular verb with collective subject "Aaron and his sons" indicates the duty falls to whichever priest serves on a given evening. *Me-erev ad-boqer* ("from evening until morning") -- the lamp burns through the night hours. *Khuqqat olam* ("a statute forever") -- a binding regulation for all future generations. *Me-et benei Yisrael* ("from the Sons of Israel") -- the oil is the community's contribution; the tending is the priesthood's duty. The LXX renders *ohel mo'ed* as *skene tou marturiou* ("tent of testimony"), interpreting *mo'ed* through the lens of *edut*; and uses *kurios* ("Lord") for YHWH.

---

*Translated from the Leningrad Codex (B19A) with reference to Codex Vaticanus (LXX). All transliterations follow standard academic conventions. Hebrew text follows the Masoretic vocalization.*
